title: Chocolate chip shortbread
subtitle: Scottish butter shortbread with dark chocolate chips
excerpt: A classic 1:2:3 shortbread enriched with finely chopped dark chocolate. The high butter content keeps the shortbread crumbly and short, with the chocolate adding texture without sweetening it into a biscuit.
sourceNotes: Shortbread is a Scottish biscuit with a documented history from at least the sixteenth century. Mary, Queen of Scots, is recorded as being fond of shortbread in the mid-sixteenth century. The 1:2:3 ratio (1 part sugar, 2 parts butter, 3 parts flour) is the standard domestic Scottish formula documented by Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). The chocolate chip addition is a twentieth-century development of that base.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: 150°C conventional / 130°C fan. Shortbread bakes low and slow to dry out rather than colour.

content.text: Shortbread is defined by its ratio: 1 part sugar to 2 parts butter to 3 parts flour by weight (100 g : 200 g : 300 g is a workable batch). The high butter content is what makes it short. Cornflour substituted for some of the plain flour makes it slightly more tender; rice flour gives a sandier texture. The chocolate should be dark (60-70% cocoa solids) and chopped small enough to stay within the biscuit rather than creating large pockets.

Do not overwork the dough: mix just until the butter and flour come together. Overworking develops gluten and makes the shortbread tough. The dough should look crumbly but press together cleanly when squeezed.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until just combined — do not aerate. Sift in the {{plain-flour}}, {{cornflour}}, and {{fine-salt}} and mix until the dough just comes together. Add the chopped {{dark-chocolate}} and press it through the dough. Shape into a flat disc, wrap, and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 150°C (130°C fan). Roll the dough out on a lightly floured surface to about 6 mm thick — thicker than most biscuits, which is what gives shortbread its depth and crumb. Cut into rounds or fingers and place on lined baking trays. Prick each biscuit several times with a fork. Chill on the tray for 15 minutes. Bake for 20-25 minutes until pale and just set: the edges should be very faintly coloured but the surface should still look uncoloured. Cool on the tray for 10 minutes, then transfer to a rack.
